content.full_text:
May 12,1992

John Torrance
Simi Valley City Attorney
2929 Tapo Canyon Road
Simi Valley, CA 93063

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
    Our File No. I-92-208

Dear Mr. Torrance:

     You have requested assistance under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Because
your request is a general inquiry, we treat it as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).2/

QUESTION

     When an official purchases a new car and trades in a car he
or she owns as credit toward the purchase price, is the trade-in
allowance considered reportable income on a statement of economic
interests?

CONCLUSION

     The trade-in allowance in this situation is not considered to
be income and, therefore, is not reportable as income on a
statement of economic interests.

ANALYSIS

     Government Code Section 82030 defines "income" as:

     . . . a payment received, including but not limited to
any salary, wage, advance, dividend interest, rent,
proceeds from any sale . . .


_________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

John Torrance
Page 2


     Where an official negotiates the purchase of a new car and,
as part of the negotiations, the car dealer credits the trade-in
value of the official's existing automobile toward the purchase
price, the trade-in value is not a "payment received" by the
official. Rather, the trade-in value is part of the purchase
price of the new car. Therefore, the trade-in allowance does not
meet the Act's definition of "income" and is not reportable on a
statement of economic interest.

     Please note, however, that any discount offered to the
official in the ultimate price paid for the new car must be made
in the regular course of business to members of the public and not
be based on the official's governmental status. Otherwise, the
amount of the discount will be a gift to the official.

     If you have any additional questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Linda Moureaux
                                           Political Reform Consultant

or she owns as credit toward the purchase price,

CONCLUSION: The trade—in allowance in this situation is not considered to
